my_thoughts: 'I'm dead to my feelings'—totalizing claim. Not 'I don't feel much' but 'I'm DEAD.' Teacher's reductio is perfect: 'cannot be psychotherapy of the dead.' Takes their metaphor literally and shows consequence: dead = no therapy possible. This is different from challenging ('you're not really dead')—instead, accepts premise and follows logic. If dead, therapy is impossible. Forces them to choose: maintain dramatic claim (lose therapy) or revise (admit aliveness). Reductio ad absurdum as teaching tool. Single turn so no response shown, but seed planted.
accumulated_insight: T1 (Single-turn): Student claims 'I'm dead to my feelings'—extreme statement presenting emotional deadness as fact. Teacher uses reductio ad absurdum: 'this cannot be psychotherapy of the dead. If you are emotionally dead, we cannot do therapy.' Logical consequence: if premise (I'm dead) is true, then therapy impossible. This forces student to either maintain claim (therapy ends) or revise (admit some aliveness). Single turn plants paradox without waiting for response.
visible_response:
But this cannot be psychotherapy of the dead.
If you are emotionally dead, then this can be only a dead therapy.
